The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Takahashi Inari Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32.7829|130.6586|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |image = [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine premises 1.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Ungraded Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Takahashi Inari Shrine}}

'''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32.7829|130.6586|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |image = [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine premises 1.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Ungraded Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Takahashi Inari Shrine}}

It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:UnRanked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Takahashi Inari Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32.7829|130.6586|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |image = [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine premises 1.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Ungraded Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. revision.text:
{{記事名の制約|title={{CP932フォント|髙}}橋稲荷神社}} {{特殊文字|説明=[[Microsoftコードページ932]]（[[はしご高]]）}} {{神社 |名称 = {{CP932フォント|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |画像 = [[画像:Takahashi-Inari-shrine premises 1.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[西区 (熊本市)|西区]]上代九丁目6-20 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = [[宇迦之御魂神]] |社格 = 旧[[無格社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建 = [[明応]]5年（[[1496年]]） |本殿 = [[神明造]] |別名 = 高橋稲荷神社 |札所等 = [[稲荷神#日本五大稲荷|日本稲荷五社]]
熊本県三大神社 |例祭 = [[11月8日]] |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kumamoto|label=高橋
稲荷神社|coordinates= {{coord2|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E}}}} }} '''高橋稲荷神社'''（たかはしいいなりじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[西区 (熊本市)|西区]]にある[[神社]]である。[[法人登記|登記]]上の[[宗教法人]]名称は高の[[異体字]]である{{CP932フォント|髙}}（はしごだか）を使用した{{CP932フォント|髙}}橋稲荷神社。 [[稲荷神#日本五大稲荷|日本稲荷五社]]{{Cite sign |author=高橋稲荷神社 |title=高橋稲荷神社 |type=案内板 |location=高橋稲荷神社 }}{{要出典範囲|他は[[伏見稲荷大社]]（[[京都府]]）、[[祐徳稲荷神社]]（[[佐賀県]]）、[[豊川稲荷]]（[[愛知県]]）、[[最上稲荷]]（[[岡山県]]）という。|date=2018年11月}}や、[[阿蘇神社]]、[[出水神社 (熊本市)|出水神社]]とともに熊本県三大神社の一つと称している。また、[[稲荷神#日本四大稲荷|日本四大稲荷]]{{Cite web|和書|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=高橋稲荷初午大祭 |publisher=藤江ホテル |date=2008-01-16 |accessdate=2018-11-21 }}や[[稲荷神#九州三大稲荷|九州三稲荷]]{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=高橋稲荷神社 熊本観光 九州 |publisher=有限会社ニューメディアエンジニアリング |accessdate=2018-11-22 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=高橋稲荷神社 |publisher=九州観光推進機構 |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=くまもと便り[熊本県内の写真] |publisher=熊本県浄化槽協会 |accessdate=2018-11-16 }}の一つに挙げられることもある。 2月[[初午]]の初午大祭には、多くの人が参詣する。 == 祭神 == [[ウカノミタマ|宇迦之御魂神]]を祀る。 == 歴史 == [[画像:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|神門]] [[明応]]5年（[[1496年]]）、[[熊本城|隈本城]]の初代城主[[鹿子木親員]]が支城として稲荷山山頂に[[上代城]]を築いた際、城内鎮守のため[[京都]]の[[伏見稲荷大社|伏見稲荷神社]]から[[稲荷神|稲荷大明神]]を[[勧請]]したのに始まる。[[天文 (元号)|天文]]10年（[[1541年]]）上代城落城の際に稲荷神社も焼失した。 [[江戸時代]]に入り、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川氏]]の[[菩提寺]]である[[海蔵寺 (熊本市)|海蔵寺]]の[[首座]]義本氏が夢で稲荷神社再興の啓示を受け、[[寛文]]元年（[[1661年]]）、現在地である稲荷山中腹に社殿を再建し遷座した。以降、熊本藩主細川氏の崇敬を受けた。 明治維新の際に高橋稲荷神社に改称した。戦後は[[神社本庁]]に参加し、[[昭和]]41年（[[1966年]]）7月1日にその[[別表神社]]とされた。 == ギャラリー == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|大鳥居 File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|神門 File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|拝殿 File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|祈祷殿 File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|奥宮へ続く鳥居 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} == 関連図書 == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|高橋稲荷神社}} * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、209頁上段 * [[鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|鈴木喬]]『熊本の神社と寺院』[[熊本日日新聞社]]、1980年、34-35頁 * 高木盛義『くまもと史跡散歩』熊本新評社、1982年、180-182頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、677頁 == 外部リンク == * {{Instagram|takahashiinarijinja}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たかはしいなりしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:稲荷神社]] [[Category:熊本県の旧無格社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]] [[category:熊本市西区の建築物]] [[Category:15世紀の日本の設立]]

[[ウカノミタマ|宇迦之御魂神]]を祀る。 == 歴史 == [[画像:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|神門]] [[明応]]5年（[[1496年]]）、[[熊本城|隈本城]]の初代城主[[鹿子木親員]]が支城として稲荷山山頂に[[上代城]]を築いた際、城内鎮守のため[[京都]]の[[伏見稲荷大社|伏見稲荷神社]]から[[稲荷神|稲荷大明神]]を[[勧請]]したのに始まる。[[天文 (元号)|天文]]10年（[[1541年]]）上代城落城の際に稲荷神社も焼失した。 [[江戸時代]]に入り、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川氏]]の[[菩提寺]]である[[海蔵寺 (熊本市)|海蔵寺]]の[[首座]]義本氏が夢で稲荷神社再興の啓示を受け、[[寛文]]元年（[[1661年]]）、現在地である稲荷山中腹に社殿を再建し遷座した。以降、熊本藩主細川氏の崇敬を受けた。 明治維新の際に高橋稲荷神社に改称した。戦後は[[神社本庁]]に参加し、[[昭和]]41年（[[1966年]]）7月1日にその[[別表神社]]とされた。 == ギャラリー == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|大鳥居 File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|神門 File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|拝殿 File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|祈祷殿 File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|奥宮へ続く鳥居 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} == 関連図書 == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|高橋稲荷神社}} * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、209頁上段 * [[鈴木喬 (歴史研究家)|鈴木喬]]『熊本の神社と寺院』[[熊本日日新聞社]]、1980年、34-35頁 * 高木盛義『くまもと史跡散歩』熊本新評社、1982年、180-182頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、677頁 == 外部リンク == * {{Instagram|takahashiinarijinja}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たかはしいなりしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:稲荷神社]] [[Category:熊本県の旧無格社]] Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Ungraded Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Ungraded Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32.7829|130.6586|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |image = [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine premises 1.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]]

'''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:熊本市西区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]]

''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市西区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32.7829|130.6586|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |image = [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine premises 1.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市西区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]]

Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市西区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]]
