Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32.7829|130.6586|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |image = [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine premises 1.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市西区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]]

Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市西区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市西区の歴史]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]]

It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11671498]] Wikidata name: Takahashi Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32.7829|130.6586|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |image = [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine premises 1.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |coordinates = {{Coord|32|46|58.8|N|130|39|30.9|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}{{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11671498]] Wikidata name: Takahashi Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7829|N|130.659|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 | location = {{ill|Kumamoto|qid=Q199889}} }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}{{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] }} [[sn:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]]Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]]Takahashi Inari Shrine]]

'''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 | location = {{ill|Kumamoto|WD=Q199889}} }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 | location = {{ill|Kumamoto|WD=Q199889}} }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. [[sn:commons:Category:Takahashi Inari-jinja]][[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]][[ja:高橋稲荷神社]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11671498]] Wikidata name: Takahashi Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7829|N|130.659|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 | location = {{ill|Kumamoto|WD=Q199889}} }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}{{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] }} [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11671498}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}{{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] }} [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11671498}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[commons:Category:Takahashi Inari-jinja]][[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]][[ja:高橋稲荷神社]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[commons:Category:Takahashi Inari-jinja]][[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]][[ja:高橋稲荷神社]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takahashi Inari-jinja]][[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]][[ja:高橋稲荷神社]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11671498]] Wikidata name: Takahashi Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7829|N|130.659|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 | location = {{ill|Kumamoto|WD=Q199889}} }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds. ''Jinja Jiten (Shrine Dictionary)'', Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 209 (upper section) * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], ''Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto)'', Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbunsha, 1980, pp. 34-35 * Moriaki Takagi, ''Kumamoto Historical Walk'', Kumamoto Shinpyosha, 1982, pp. 180-182 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee, ed. ''Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary 43 Kumamoto Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, p. 677 {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{Japan-shrine-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ==See also== *[[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] {{commons Category|Takahashi_Inari-jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}{{Shinto-stub}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|高橋稲荷神社|version=102021348|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:15th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1496 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1490s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] }} [[ja:高橋稲荷神社]] [[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takahashi Inari-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11671498}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[commons:Category:Takahashi Inari-jinja]][[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]][[ja:高橋稲荷神社]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[commons:Category:Takahashi Inari-jinja]][[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]][[ja:高橋稲荷神社]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takahashi Inari-jinja]][[en:Takahashi Inari Shrine]][[ja:高橋稲荷神社]][[simple:Takahashi Inari Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11671498]] Wikidata name: Takahashi Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Takahashi Inari Shrine'''|高橋稲荷神社||}} | image = File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG | caption = Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|32.7829|N|130.659|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社 |location = Kumamoto Prefecture, Kumamoto City, Nishi Ward, Kamishiro 9-chome 6-20 |deity = Ukanomitama no Kami |Status = Former Unranked Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |established = Meiō 5 (1496) |Main hall = Shinmei-zukuri |Other name = Takahashi Inari Shrine |Notable features = One of the Five Great Inari Shrines in Japan
One of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture |festivals = November 8 | location = {{ill|Kumamoto|WD=Q199889}} }} '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (高橋稲荷神社, ''Takahashi inari jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]], dedicated to the worship of the [[kami]] [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]]. It is located in [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. Its main festival is held annually on November 8. It was founded in 1496. '''Takahashi Inari Shrine''' (たかはしいいなりじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Nishi Ward, Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]]. The legally registered religious corporation name uses the ladder form of "Taka" as {{CP932 font|髙}} (Hashigodaka), so the official name is {{CP932 font|髙}}橋稲荷神社. It is considered one of the [[Inari Shrine#Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Five Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite sign |author=Takahashi Inari Shrine |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |type=Signboard |location=Takahashi Inari Shrine }}{{Citation needed|Other shrines include [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] (Kyoto Prefecture), [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Saga Prefecture), [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]] (Aichi Prefecture), and [[Saijō Inari]] (Okayama Prefecture)|date=November 2018}}, and alongside [[Aso Shrine]] and [[Izumi Shrine (Kumamoto City)|Izumi Shrine]], it is called one of the Three Great Shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture. It is also sometimes listed among the [[Inari Shrine#Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan|Four Great Inari Shrines of Japan]]{{Cite web|url=https://plaza.rakuten.co.jp/fujie/diary/200801150000/ |title=Takahashi Inari First Horse festival |publisher=Fujie Hotel |date=January 16, 2008 |accessdate=November 21, 2018 }} and the [[Inari Shrine#Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu|Three Great Inari Shrines of Kyushu]]{{Cite web|url=http://www.ajkj.jp/ajkj/kumamoto/kumamoto/kanko/tahakashiinari/takahashiinari.html |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine Kumamoto Tourism Kyushu |publisher=New Media Engineering Co., Ltd. |accessdate=November 22, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=https://www.welcomekyushu.jp/event/?mode=detail&id=9999902006738 |title=Takahashi Inari Shrine |publisher=Kyushu Tourism Promotion Organization |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}{{Cite web|url=http://johkasou.jp/modules/page/index.php?&cid=10 |title=Kumamoto News [Photos in Kumamoto Prefecture] |publisher=Kumamoto Prefecture Septic Tank Association |accessdate=November 16, 2018 }}. Many visitors come to the shrine for the First Horse festival in February. == deity == The shrine enshrines [[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Kami]]. == History == [[File:Takahashi-Inari-shrine gateway 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Shinmon (God Gate)]] In Meiō 5 (1496), Kanokogi Chikakazu, the first lord of [[:en:Kumamoto Castle|Kumamoto Castle]] (then known as Kumamoto Castle), built Kamishiro Castle on top of Inariyama mountain and established the shrine to enshrine Inari Daimyōjin from [[:en:Fushimi Inari Taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] in Kyoto for the castle's protection. The shrine was burned down when Kamishiro Castle fell in Tenbun 10 (1541). In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the head monk of [[Kaizō-ji]] temple, the family temple of the [[:en:Hosokawa clan|Hosokawa clan]], had a revelation in a dream to rebuild the shrine. In Kanbun 1 (1661), the shrine was rebuilt on its current location on the mountainside of Inariyama and was patronized by the Hosokawa clan. During the Meiji Restoration, the shrine was renamed Takahashi Inari Shrine. After World War II, the shrine joined the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and was designated a Beppyo Shrine on July 1, 1966. == Gallery == File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine6.JPG|Large Torii Gate File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine2.JPG|Shinmon (God Gate) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine3.JPG|Haiden (Worship Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine5.JPG|Kaidōden (Prayer Hall) File:Kumamoto Takahashi Inari Shrine4.JPG|Torii leading to Okunomiya (Inner Shrine) == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{commonscat|Takahashi Inari-jinja|Takahashi Inari Shrine}} * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, eds.
